name: Woodland Edge
subtitle: Where the woods meet the open
summary: The graded, layered band where closed forest gives way to open ground — dappled, shifting light, rich leaf-littered soil, and a full stack of layers from canopy to groundcover. Among the busiest and most plantable ground in any landscape.

layers.note: Tall trees that close overhead behind the edge — oaks, maples, hickories — casting the shade the inner-woodland plants depend on.
Smaller trees beneath the canopy — serviceberry, dogwood, redbud, hornbeam — catching the light the big trees let through.
Woody shrubs forming the edge's outer wall — viburnums, spicebush, hazelnut — dense cover for nesting birds and shelter for small animals.
Wildflowers and grasses of part shade — wild geranium, columbine, golden alexanders — the showy spring-into-summer ribbon along the edge.
Low, shade-tolerant plants and leaf litter carpeting the soil — wild ginger, woodland sedges — holding moisture and covering bare ground.

name: Oak Savanna
subtitle: Scattered oaks over open grassland
summary: Widely spaced, open-grown oaks standing over a continuous prairie ground layer — a sunlit community historically kept open by periodic fire.

layers.note: Widely spaced oaks — bur oak and white oak are characteristic; crowns rarely touch.
Sparse and intermittent — scattered small trees in gaps rather than a closed tier.
Low and fire-pruned; clonal shrubs that resprout after disturbance, kept patchy.
The heart of the savanna — warm-season grasses and sun-loving forbs, continuous beneath the oaks.
Grass thatch, sedges, and fallen oak leaves — the fine fuel that historically carried ground fires.

designPrinciples: Mimic fire with mowing or cutting
